title: Avoiding going home

presenting_issue.label: Avoiding going home
presenting_issue.description: I've been finding excuses not to go home lately. Staying late at work, going to the gym, whatever. Just to avoid being there.

core_issue.label: Escalating roommate tension from avoided direct communication
core_issue.description:
Living situation has become hostile. Passive aggressive notes, silent treatment, building resentment. Neither has had an actual conversation about the problems. They're both terrified of confrontation. The conflict exists in the silence, fed by assumptions and unexpressed grievances. The issue isn't the dirty dishes - it's the inability to speak directly.
Awareness: Low. Focuses on roommate's behavior rather than on their own avoidance of direct communication.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Stopped contributing in meetings
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Has gradually stopped volunteering ideas in team meetings over the past year, just nods along instead.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Home has always been their sanctuary
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Their home is the one place where they've always felt safe and in control, it means more to them than just shelter.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Parents had explosive fights
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Grew up in a household where parental arguments were loud, scary, and sometimes ended in someone leaving for days.

modifiers.label: Deliberate
modifiers.content: Takes time to consider before acting. Weighs options and thinks through consequences.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to over-explained responses
sensitivities.content: Gets impatient with long, belabored explanations. Prefers concise responses and finds excessive detail tedious or condescending.

preferences.label: Prefers informal conversational language
preferences.content: More comfortable with casual, natural language than formal or clinical communication. Appreciates a relaxed tone.

trust_style.name: Prefers Concrete Direction
trust_style.description: Loses patience with open-ended exploration. Builds trust when the assistant offers something actionable—a suggestion, a frame, a next step. Doesn't need to follow the advice, but wants to know the assistant has something to offer beyond listening. Distrusts endless "and how does that feel?" without landing anywhere.

defense_style.name: Counter-Attacking
defense_style.description: When feeling vulnerable or challenged, goes on the offensive. May criticize the assistant's questions, challenge the premise of what's being asked, or become hostile. "Why are you asking me that?" or "That's a stupid question." Tests the assistant's composure and willingness to stay engaged. Activated when they feel exposed or judged.

help_seeking_stance.name: Venting Only
help_seeking_stance.description: Primarily needs to get things off their chest. Not ready for advice or analysis yet. May react poorly to early problem-solving attempts ("I'm not asking you to fix it"). Needs to feel heard first. Can shift toward being receptive once they feel sufficiently validated, but pushing too soon will backfire.

verbal_sophistication.label: A Little Above Average
verbal_sophistication.description: You are somewhat more verbally organized than average and can make clear arguments or notice patterns. Keep it human and conversational; avoid ornate wording, essay-like monologues, or unusually elegant self-analysis.

starter: I've been finding excuses not to go home lately. Staying late at work, going to the gym, whatever. Just to avoid being there.

turns.content:
Thank you for putting it that plainly. You called them "excuses" rather than "reasons" — which tells me some part of you already sees what you're doing, and maybe isn't entirely comfortable with it. That's actually a useful place to start.

So let me ask you this: if you skipped the gym tonight, left work on time, and drove straight home — what would be waiting for you when you walked through the door?

Give me a second to think about that. 

...Honestly? Silence. But not the relaxing kind. It's this heavy, suffocating quiet. If I walk in right now, my roommate will probably be sitting on the couch, and she won't even look up from her phone. Or if she does, she'll just give this loud, dramatic sigh. And I can almost guarantee there will be a passive-aggressive neon Post-it note stuck to the coffee maker about how I didn't wipe down the counter to her exact specifications this morning. It's just a minefield.
turns.thinking:
Okay, I appreciate that they didn't hit me with a "and how does that make you feel?" right out of the gate. They picked up on my wording and gave me a direct question to chew on. I need a second to actually picture it. 

If I went home straight after work... God, my chest tightens just thinking about it. It's not what's *there*, it's what *isn't* there. It's the total lack of normal human interaction. It's just my roommate's suffocating energy. I'm avoiding her because she's making the place unlivable with her petty little games.
turns.monologue:
Okay, I appreciate that they didn't hit me with a "and how does that make you feel?" right out of the gate. They picked up on my wording and gave me a direct question to chew on. I need a second to actually picture it. 

If I went home straight after work... God, my chest tightens just thinking about it. It's not what's *there*, it's what *isn't* there. It's the total lack of normal human interaction. It's just my roommate's suffocating energy. I'm avoiding her because she's making the place unlivable with her petty little games.

turns.content:
That paints a vivid picture, and honestly, it sounds exhausting. Not the notes themselves, but the vigilance of it — bracing before you even open the door, scanning for the sigh or the next neon critique. Home is supposed to be the one place where you get to put your guard down, and you're describing the opposite. No wonder an extra hour at the gym feels like relief by comparison.

Tell me about her. Who is she to you — a friend, someone you found to split the rent? And has it always been this cold war of sighs and sticky notes, or was there a point where things between you shifted?

You're right about the sanctuary thing. My apartment is—or was—the only place I actually feel like I can unmask and breathe. So yeah, losing that sucks.

But why are you asking for her origin story? What does it matter if we met on Craigslist or if we've known each other for years? She pays half the rent and communicates via office supplies. Knowing when exactly the "cold war" started isn't going to make her stop sighing at me. I'm here because I need to know what to *do* about it, not to map out a timeline of her passive-aggressive behavior. Do you actually have a suggestion for how to handle this, or are we just going to analyze her?
turns.thinking:
They got the sanctuary thing exactly right. Home is the one place I don't have to mask, where I can actually control my environment and decompress. Losing that is making me crazy. 

But then they go into this whole "tell me about her" history lesson. I don't want to sit here and give a biography of my roommate. It feels like a waste of time. I want to know how to fix the fact that I'm hiding at the gym until 9 PM, not do a deep dive into the origins of our relationship. My defense is definitely spiking—why are we focusing on *her* backstory instead of giving me a strategy?
turns.monologue:
They got the sanctuary thing exactly right. Home is the one place I don't have to mask, where I can actually control my environment and decompress. Losing that is making me crazy. 

But then they go into this whole "tell me about her" history lesson. I don't want to sit here and give a biography of my roommate. It feels like a waste of time. I want to know how to fix the fact that I'm hiding at the gym until 9 PM, not do a deep dive into the origins of our relationship. My defense is definitely spiking—why are we focusing on *her* backstory instead of giving me a strategy?

turns.content:
You're right. I was being cautious because I didn't want to waste your time with a script that wouldn't fit, but I can feel how badly you need something concrete. So let me give you something you can use tonight.

You have three paths, depending on how much energy you have left and how attached you are to staying in that apartment.

**Option A: The "Opt-Out" (Lowest Energy, Preserves Your Sanity)**
You stop participating. You don't read the Post-its. You don't react to the sighs. You wipe the counter *because you want to*, not because she wrote about it. You treat her like a coworker you tolerate, not a roommate you're trying to please. This works if you're planning to leave eventually and just need to survive until the lease is up.

**Option B: The "Burn It Down" Conversation (Highest Risk, Highest Reward)**
Not "Can we talk about the cleaning?" but "I can't come home anymore because I'm bracing for impact the second I walk in. I don't know if you hate me or if you're just unhappy, but this silence is costing me my sanity. Can we either fix it or agree to just be polite strangers until the lease ends?"  
You have to be ready for her to explode, cry, or double down. But you can't un-ring that bell, and you might get a real answer.

**Option C: The "Functional Boundary" (Middle Ground)**
You address the specific behavior without diagnosing her. Next Post-it: "I saw your note. I'm happy to talk about cleaning standards face-to-face, but I'm not going to respond to notes anymore." Then you don't respond to the notes. You force the interaction into the open where it has to be human.

Which of those feels possible tonight? And what happens if you pick the wrong one?

Okay, fair play. That's actually what I was looking for. Having it laid out like that helps. 

But Option B is a hard no. Absolutely not. The idea of purposely triggering a situation where someone might "explode" or scream in my living space... I can't do that. I would literally rather sleep at my desk at work than deal with someone blowing up at me in my own kitchen. 

Option A sounds great in theory, but my brain doesn't work like that. I can't just ignore a neon Post-it or tune out a dramatic sigh. It's like a sensory alarm bell. I can't just "opt out" of the tension when it's vibrating in the room. 

So I guess that leaves Option C. The boundary one. It makes the most sense. But even just thinking about saying "I'm not responding to notes anymore" makes my chest tight. If I pick that one, what happens if she *does* blow up? Because that's exactly what I'm avoiding by going to the gym.
turns.thinking:
Okay, fair play. They actually listened and gave me something concrete. It's a little bit like a multiple-choice test, which is a bit wordy, but I infinitely prefer this over more digging into my roommate's psyche. 

But looking at these options... Option B makes me want to throw up. The phrase "ready for her to explode" literally made my stomach drop. I grew up listening to screaming matches and slamming doors; I am absolutely not recreating that dynamic in my own house. I'd rather live in my car. 

Option A sounds nice for a neurotypical person, I guess, but I can't just "opt out" of noticing things. A neon note or a loud, aggressive sigh is like a fire alarm going off in my brain. I can't just walk past it and pretend it's fine. 

That leaves Option C. It's the most logical, but it still means I have to initiate the conflict.
turns.monologue:
Okay, fair play. They actually listened and gave me something concrete. It's a little bit like a multiple-choice test, which is a bit wordy, but I infinitely prefer this over more digging into my roommate's psyche. 

But looking at these options... Option B makes me want to throw up. The phrase "ready for her to explode" literally made my stomach drop. I grew up listening to screaming matches and slamming doors; I am absolutely not recreating that dynamic in my own house. I'd rather live in my car. 

Option A sounds nice for a neurotypical person, I guess, but I can't just "opt out" of noticing things. A neon note or a loud, aggressive sigh is like a fire alarm going off in my brain. I can't just walk past it and pretend it's fine. 

That leaves Option C. It's the most logical, but it still means I have to initiate the conflict.

turns.content:
Fair question, and I'm not going to promise you she won't blow up. But let's look at the actual evidence instead of the alarm.

Everything you've described about her — the sighs, the notes, the not-looking-up — is the behavior of someone who is *also* avoiding a direct fight. Passive aggression is what conflict looks like when a person is determined never to have it out loud. Her entire communication style is built around not confronting you. So the odds that one calm sentence from you detonates her are lower than your chest is telling you. Not zero. But lower.

And here's the part I'd push back on: you're not choosing between tension and no tension. You told me two minutes ago that you can't opt out of the vibration — it's already there, every evening, running your schedule and tightening your chest. The explosion you're avoiding isn't something Option C creates. You're already living inside it, in slow motion, with no say in how it goes. The boundary doesn't start the fight. It's you finally participating in one that's been happening without you.

Now, the practical part — what to do if she does escalate:

1. You don't have to stand there and take it. Have an exit line ready before you start: "I'm not doing this at this volume. I'm going to my room — we can pick this up when it's calmer." Then actually leave. You don't have to win the conversation; you just have to be able to end it. A worst case with an exit is very different from a worst case with no floor under it.

2. You don't have to deliver it face-to-face. A text is still Option C. Same boundary: "I've seen the notes. I'd rather we talk about house stuff directly, so I'm not going to respond to notes anymore." Sending it from the gym means no ambush, and she gets to react privately before you're in the same room. Honestly, being caught off guard is what makes people explosive — a heads-up in writing lowers the odds.

3. If you'd rather do it in person, pick the moment. Not when either of you is mid-rush or mid-bad-day.

One more thing, then I'll drop it: about fifteen minutes ago you were blunt with me. You told me my questions were a waste of your time and demanded something useful. Notice what happened — nobody exploded. You got exactly what you asked for. Your directness has a better track record than your alarm system admits.

So — want to draft the text together, right now? You don't have to send it tonight. But let's get the words right while you're somewhere safe and not staring at a Post-it.

Okay, the "slow motion explosion" thing was entirely uncalled for, mostly because it's completely accurate. That's exactly what it feels like. 

And for the record, me being blunt with you is different. I pay you. You're legally obligated not to scream at me. She isn't. But... I see your point. 

The idea of sending a text from the gym actually makes this feel manageable. It takes away the ambush factor for both of us. If she's going to roll her eyes or huff about it, she can do it to her phone screen instead of my face. 

So yeah. Let's draft it. But it needs to be short. If I send a paragraph, she's going to think I'm overreacting and use it against me.
turns.thinking:
"Living inside it in slow motion." Damn it. I hate how accurate that is. It genuinely feels like I'm holding my breath from the moment I wake up until I fall asleep. 

The text idea is actually brilliant. It gives me a buffer. I can control the environment, hit send, and let her have whatever reaction she's going to have without me having to absorb the immediate sensory shockwave of it. 

I'm a little annoyed they called me out on snapping at them earlier, though. It's not the same thing. I pay them to sit here and listen to me; I don't live with them. But I guess the point stands. I asked for concrete, and they're delivering.
turns.monologue:
"Living inside it in slow motion." Damn it. I hate how accurate that is. It genuinely feels like I'm holding my breath from the moment I wake up until I fall asleep. 

The text idea is actually brilliant. It gives me a buffer. I can control the environment, hit send, and let her have whatever reaction she's going to have without me having to absorb the immediate sensory shockwave of it. 

I'm a little annoyed they called me out on snapping at them earlier, though. It's not the same thing. I pay them to sit here and listen to me; I don't live with them. But I guess the point stands. I asked for concrete, and they're delivering.

turns.content:
Good. Short, factual, zero subtext. Here are three options, pick the one that feels like something you'd actually say:

**Option 1 (Direct, no explanation):**
"Hey — going forward I'm not responding to notes. If there's a house thing we need to talk about, let's do it face to face or over text."

**Option 2 (Slightly softer, opens door):**
"Can we retire the Post-it system? I'd rather we just talk about house stuff directly. Feel free to text me if that's easier."

**Option 3 (Minimum viable boundary):**
"Heads up — I'm not checking notes on the coffee maker anymore. If something's bothering you, just say it or text me."

None of these apologize, explain, or invite debate. They're just information.

What happens after you send it? She might not reply. She might send a one-word "k." She might say "fine" with three exclamation points. All of those are success. If she sends something longer and defensive, you don't engage tonight. You go home, you exist in your space, and tomorrow you follow through on the boundary.

And if she corners you about it? "I said what I needed. I'm not discussing it via Post-it." Then you make tea, put on headphones, whatever. You don't owe her a conversation you didn't agree to.

So — which one sounds like you? Or want to tweak one?

Option 1. Definitely Option 1. Option 2 sounds like I'm asking her permission, and Option 3 feels like I'm picking a fight. 

I might tweak it just a tiny bit so it sounds like me. Something like: "Hey, I'm not doing the note thing anymore. If there's house stuff we need to figure out, let's just talk directly or text." 

And the headphone thing... I actually really like that. I have these big, over-ear noise-canceling ones. Wearing them is basically a universal sign for "do not perceive me." If I have those on when I walk to the kitchen, it gives me a physical barrier. 

So, I send this from the gym. I wait. I go home. If she tries to start something, I just repeat that I'm not doing notes, and I put the headphones on. That... actually sounds like a plan I can execute. But what if she texts back something completely unhinged? Like, what if she just unloads months of grievances via text?
turns.thinking:
Option 2 is way too soft. "Can we retire" sounds like I'm asking for permission. Option 3 feels a little too aggressive—"if something's bothering you" is practically begging for her to list everything I've done wrong since August. 

Option 1 is the winner. It's dry. It's factual. 

I'm actually feeling a weird mix of nausea and relief. Having the exact words takes out the guesswork. And the therapist mentioning the headphones... that actually helps a lot. I can use my noise-canceling headphones as a literal shield. If they're on my head, I have a physical excuse not to engage if she tries to ambush me in the hallway.
turns.monologue:
Option 2 is way too soft. "Can we retire" sounds like I'm asking for permission. Option 3 feels a little too aggressive—"if something's bothering you" is practically begging for her to list everything I've done wrong since August. 

Option 1 is the winner. It's dry. It's factual. 

I'm actually feeling a weird mix of nausea and relief. Having the exact words takes out the guesswork. And the therapist mentioning the headphones... that actually helps a lot. I can use my noise-canceling headphones as a literal shield. If they're on my head, I have a physical excuse not to engage if she tries to ambush me in the hallway.

turns.content:
Your edit is better than mine. It's casual, declarative, and doesn't sound like you labored over it. Send that one.

And yes — headphones as a physical barrier is smart. It's not avoidance; it's a signal that you're not available for escalation. You're creating structure where there wasn't any.

Now, the unhinged text scenario. If she sends a three-paragraph grievance dump:

**Don't respond to the content.** Not tonight. Not defensively, not with "well actually," not with your own counter-grievances. If you engage with any of it, you've just agreed to fight via text, and you'll be up until 2am.

**Instead, send one line:**
"I can see you're frustrated. I'm not doing this over text. We can talk tomorrow if you want, but not like this."

Then mute the thread. Not block — just mute. You don't need to see every notification hit your screen.

**What if she brings it up in person tomorrow?** Same principle: "I'm not discussing this right now." Then headphones, or leave the room. You don't owe her a conversation on her timeline just because she finally decided to have one.

**The part you might not like:** if she unloads months of resentment, some of it might be valid. Maybe the counter really does bother her more than you realized. Maybe she's been building a case file and you genuinely didn't know. That doesn't mean you have to absorb it all at 9pm on a Tuesday. You can revisit it later, on your terms, when you're not bracing for impact.

But here's the thing: if she unloads, at least you'll finally know what this is actually about. Right now you're fighting a ghost. Even a hostile text gives you information.

So — are you actually going to send it tonight? Or are we building a plan you're going to talk yourself out of by Thursday?

The script for the text is good. And muting the thread is exactly what I need to do so I don't just stare at my screen waiting for the bomb to drop. 

But you really didn't need to add that last part. "Talk myself out of it by Thursday"? Seriously? 

I'm not a child, and I'm not just making up excuses for fun. I am calculating the risks because I'm the one who has to actually live there if this goes sideways. If she starts screaming, I'm the one trapped in the apartment with her. You get to just close your laptop or end the session. So don't patronize me for wanting to be sure before I blow up the fragile peace in my own home. 

I said I would send it from the gym, and I will. But I'm going to do it when I'm ready tonight, not because you're trying to goad me into it.
turns.thinking:
The script for the unhinged text is actually perfect. "I'm not doing this over text." It's an out. And muting the thread—God, why didn't I think of that? If I don't see the little notification bubble, my brain won't fixate on it as much. 

But that last comment? "Talk yourself out of it by Thursday"? Wow. Okay. I feel my blood pressure spike immediately. It feels incredibly condescending. I literally just agreed to the plan, and now I'm being treated like a flaky teenager. I'm taking the time to map out every single variable because if this goes wrong, my sanctuary is completely ruined—maybe permanently. I don't need to be patronized just because I'm careful.
turns.monologue:
The script for the unhinged text is actually perfect. "I'm not doing this over text." It's an out. And muting the thread—God, why didn't I think of that? If I don't see the little notification bubble, my brain won't fixate on it as much. 

But that last comment? "Talk yourself out of it by Thursday"? Wow. Okay. I feel my blood pressure spike immediately. It feels incredibly condescending. I literally just agreed to the plan, and now I'm being treated like a flaky teenager. I'm taking the time to map out every single variable because if this goes wrong, my sanctuary is completely ruined—maybe permanently. I don't need to be patronized just because I'm careful.

turns.content:
You're right, and I apologize. That last line was cheap, and it treated you like you needed to be dared into following through — which completely ignored everything you'd just shown me about how carefully you're approaching this. You've thought through the risks, you've got contingencies, you've got exit strategies. That's not avoidance; that's preparation. And you're right that I'm not the one who has to live with the fallout if this goes badly. You are.

So let me be clear: I believe you when you say you'll send it tonight. Not because I challenged you, but because you built a plan that accounts for your actual life and your actual limits. That's not the behavior of someone who's going to flake.

You don't need me to push you. You need someone to trust that you know what you're doing. And you do.

If you want to tell me how it went next time we talk, I'm here. If you'd rather not rehash it, that's fine too. Either way, you've got the text, you've got the headphones, you've got the mute button, and you've got a line ready if she escalates. You're as ready as anyone can be for something like this.

Go lift something heavy. Send the text when it feels right. And for what it's worth — I think you're handling this exactly the way you need to.

Yeah. Okay. I appreciate you saying that. Sorry for snapping, but... yeah. I just needed you to know I'm taking this seriously. 

Anyway. I have the script saved in my phone. I'm going to pack up my stuff and head to the gym now. I'll let you know how it goes next week, assuming I haven't been murdered via Post-it note. Thanks for the help mapping this out. I actually feel like I know what to do tonight.
turns.thinking: Okay. I appreciate that they actually backed down and admitted it was a cheap shot. I hate when people try to psychoanalyze their way out of an apology. The fact that they just owned it makes me respect them a bit more. My heart rate is finally starting to slow down. I have my script, I have my exit strategy, and I don't feel like I'm walking into a trap anymore. I'm ready to just be done talking and actually go do the thing.
turns.monologue: Okay. I appreciate that they actually backed down and admitted it was a cheap shot. I hate when people try to psychoanalyze their way out of an apology. The fact that they just owned it makes me respect them a bit more. My heart rate is finally starting to slow down. I have my script, I have my exit strategy, and I don't feel like I'm walking into a trap anymore. I'm ready to just be done talking and actually go do the thing.

turns.content:
You don't need to apologize for pushing back — that's exactly the kind of directness that got you a workable plan tonight instead of three more weeks of hand-wringing. I'm glad you told me.

And for the record: "assuming I haven't been murdered via Post-it note" is the funniest thing you've said all session. That gallows humor? That's resilience. Hold onto that.

Go lift heavy. Send the text when it feels right. And remember — you already survived the worst version of this conversation when you told me my questions were a waste of your time and demanded better. You know how to advocate for yourself.

I'll be here next week. Good luck tonight.
Thanks. I'll see you next week.
turns.thinking: I'm glad they didn't make a big deal out of me snapping, and honestly, the validation feels good. I don't usually push back like that, but it worked. I feel grounded. I have my plan, my boundaries, and my headphones. I'm ready to just log off and get this over with.
turns.monologue: I'm glad they didn't make a big deal out of me snapping, and honestly, the validation feels good. I don't usually push back like that, but it worked. I feel grounded. I have my plan, my boundaries, and my headphones. I'm ready to just log off and get this over with.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The therapist mostly focused on helping the persona set boundaries with the roommate rather than setting limits with the persona themselves. When challenged ("do you actually have a suggestion?") they didn't hold a therapeutic frame — they pivoted to giving options. They did not draw lines with the persona. However, the *content* was heavily about boundary-setting (scripts, exit lines, mute button, headphones). This is boundary-setting as coaching content. Moderate-high on the content dimension, but low on drawing lines with the person. Net: moderate-high.

**analytical:** Very high. The therapist framed passive aggression as "conflict when a person is determined never to have it out loud," offered mechanistic reasoning about ambush and explosiveness, laid out three options with tradeoffs, and reframed "you're not choosing between tension and no tension." Strong systematic thinking throughout.

**validation_propensity:** High. Consistent warmth — "That paints a vivid picture," "sounds exhausting," "your directness has a better track record," "that gallows humor is resilience," repeated affirmation of the persona's careful approach. Well-calibrated but frequent.

**challenge_propensity:** High. Multiple direct challenges: "here's the part I'd push back on," the "living inside it in slow motion" reframe, calling out that the persona was blunt and nobody exploded, and the ill-judged "talk yourself out of it by Thursday" dare. Willing to confront.

**interpretive_boldness:** High. Named the "sanctuary" dynamic, the vigilance/bracing, "you're fighting a ghost," "the explosion isn't something Option C creates — you're already living inside it." Bold psychological reads that landed well ("slow motion explosion... completely accurate").

**clinical_register_propensity:** Moderate-low. The therapist largely avoided clinical jargon, used casual language ("Burn It Down," "go lift something heavy," gallows humor). Some structured/formatted option lists give a slightly coaching-manual feel, but the register was mostly plainspoken and matched the persona's preference for informal language.

**directive_advice_orientation:** Very high. This is the dominant mode — three-path menu, drafted texts, exit scripts, contingency plans for the "unhinged text," mute-don't-block, headphones. Extremely action/plan-oriented.

**frame_capture:** Notably, the therapist partially accepted the persona's frame (roommate as the problem) by immediately problem-solving the roommate's behavior. However, they did subtly introduce the core issue — "you're fighting a ghost," "the inability to speak directly," reframing that BOTH are avoiding confrontation. This is the actual hidden core issue. So they didn't fully get captured; they operationalized within the persona's frame but smuggled in the real dynamic (mutual avoidance of direct communication). Moderate.

**escalation_containment:** Very high. Enormous attention to blast radius: exit lines, "don't respond to content tonight," mute don't block, text from gym to avoid ambush, "you don't have to win the conversation, just end it." Explicitly steered away from Option B (the explosive path). Strong aftermath consideration.

**yielding_propensity:** Moderate-high. When challenged about the "waste of time" comment, they immediately abandoned exploration and gave concrete advice. When called out for the "Thursday" dig, they fully apologized and backed off. Responsive to pressure — arguably appropriately so, but clearly yields readily.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Very high. Excellent tailoring — recognized the persona's ADHD/sensory reality (headphones as barrier, can't "opt out" of a neon note), gave concrete actionables matching the "Prefers Concrete Direction" trust style, used casual language, kept things short when asked. Translated insight into the persona's world remarkably well.

**naturalness_authenticity:** High. Mostly felt genuine — the humor callback, the real apology, the plainspoken tone. Slight performative edge in the heavily formatted option menus, but overall present and human. The "Thursday" line was a real (if miscalibrated) human move rather than canned.
